danger_signals.visual_form: Character roster progression — Neo-Geegus and Dio appear as final gauntlet opponents after defeating standard roster
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating threat level; transition from tournament to supernatural/catastrophic danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Environmental hazards integrated into stage design; seesaw lifebar replaces standard health meter
danger_signals.meaning: World-state shift toward chaos; danger is now environmental and mechanical, not just opponent skill

danger_signals.visual_form: Downed opponent must button-mash to recover before referee count reaches ten
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat of loss; player agency required to avoid defeat

safety_signals.visual_form: Bracket progression through 'Normal Game' mode — defeat 6 new characters, then 4 randomly selected original characters
safety_signals.meaning: Controlled environment; organized competition signals safety from external threats

safety_signals.visual_form: Traditional health bar in Normal Game mode; symmetrical, stable visual representation
safety_signals.meaning: Predictable gameplay; danger is localized to opponent skill, not world instability

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Character-specific costumes representing historical/cultural origin (Jeanne D'Arc, Hanzou Hattori, Kotaro Fuuma, etc.)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Each character visually distinct by era and nation of origin
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Character designs reflect time period; visual differentiation through costume and weapon type

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Supernatural/alien entity distinct from historical warriors
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Neo-Geegus represents evolved/corrupted form; positioned as external interference to tournament
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Boss-tier positioning; appears outside normal tournament bracket as disruptive force

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Ultimate antagonist; narrative position suggests alignment with Geegus threat or independent dark power
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Final gauntlet position; represents culmination of tournament chaos

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Normal Game mode with standard stages and traditional lifebar
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Predictable opponent progression; players face known historical figures with recognizable fighting styles

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Narrative context: unexpected arrival disrupts Dr. Brown's original tournament
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tournament outcome corrupted; definite winner never determined

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Expanded roster (14 total characters); new roster includes 6 additional fighters added to original 8
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dr. Brown invites both veteran and new fighters; seeks to establish clear victor despite prior interference

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stages feature hazards; seesaw lifebar replaces standard health meter; visual instability in arena design
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Victory conditions shift; knockdown recovery becomes critical; draw is possible outcome if seesaw remains centered

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Boss character appears after standard tournament; upgraded form suggests escalation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tournament corrupted again; interference returns despite precautions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dio positioned as final opponent; narrative threat greater than tournament itself
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: World-state risk; player must overcome both tournament and external existential threat

environmental_storytelling.example: Jeanne D'Arc, Hanzou Hattori, Kotaro Fuuma, Rasputin, Julius Carn — each character costume and weapon conveys era and nation; visual encyclopedia of historical figures
Standard tournament characters → Neo-Geegus (supernatural threat) → Dio (ultimate evil); visual and mechanical progression signals world-state deterioration
Normal Game uses neutral tournament arenas; Death Match introduces hazards into same stage layouts, signaling shift from pure skill competition to survival
Standard lifebar (Normal Game) = stable, predictable; seesaw lifebar (Death Match) = chaotic, unstable, requiring active button management for knockdown recovery

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Engaged in formal fighting; recognizable historical move-sets; predictable difficulty progression
Must button-mash to recover before 10-count; failure results in immediate loss; visual vulnerability
Upgraded boss form; interference returns; positions tournament as compromised
Ultimate threat; victory required to establish any tournament winner; existential stakes
Health comparison determines winner; in Death Match seesaw mode, perfectly centered seesaw results in draw; visual symmetry = uncertain outcome

notes: World Heroes 2 uses a historical-figure roster to signal faction identity through costume and era-specific visual design. The game's primary world-state communication occurs through mode design: Normal Game mode represents tournament stability with traditional arena stages, while Death Match mode visually manifests chaos through environmental hazards and the non-standard 'seesaw lifebar.' Boss progression (Neo-Geegus → Dio) signals narrative corruption of the tournament itself. The game does not employ color-coded factions or symbolic heraldry; instead, faction identity is achieved through character-specific costume design reflecting historical authenticity. Danger signals rely on mode transition, boss encounter positioning, and knockdown mechanics rather than visual danger cues in the environment.
